title: JOINs
description: Combine rows from two or more tables.

lessons.title: INNER JOIN
lessons.content:
An `INNER JOIN` returns only rows with matches in both tables.

```sql
SELECT o.id, c.name
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id;
```

`JOIN` alone defaults to `INNER JOIN` in most dialects.

lessons.title: LEFT, RIGHT, and FULL OUTER JOIN
lessons.content:
**LEFT JOIN** keeps all rows from the left table, filling unmatched right-side columns with `NULL`:
```sql
SELECT c.name, o.id
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id;
```
This finds customers with **no orders** (`o.id IS NULL`) — a very common interview pattern.

**RIGHT JOIN** is the mirror image (rare in practice — usually rewritten as a LEFT JOIN with tables swapped).

**FULL OUTER JOIN** keeps unmatched rows from both sides.

lessons.title: Self joins
lessons.content:
A self join relates a table to itself — common for hierarchical data like employee/manager relationships:
```sql
SELECT e.name AS employee, m.name AS manager
FROM employees e
LEFT JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id;
```

title: GROUP BY & Aggregates
description: Summarize data with COUNT, SUM, AVG, and GROUP BY.

lessons.title: Aggregate functions
lessons.content:
Aggregate functions operate over groups of rows: `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`.

```sql
SELECT department, COUNT(*) AS headcount, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
GROUP BY department;
```

Every non-aggregated column in the `SELECT` list must appear in `GROUP BY`, or the query is invalid in most dialects.

lessons.title: HAVING vs WHERE
lessons.content:
`WHERE` filters rows **before** grouping; `HAVING` filters groups **after** aggregation.

```sql
SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE hire_date > '2020-01-01'
GROUP BY department
HAVING AVG(salary) > 70000;
```

This is one of the most frequently tested distinctions in SQL interviews.

lessons.title: Subqueries in WHERE
lessons.content:
A subquery returns a result used by the outer query:
```sql
SELECT name FROM employees
WHERE department_id IN (
  SELECT id FROM departments WHERE location = 'NYC'
);
```

lessons.title: Correlated subqueries
lessons.content:
A correlated subquery references a column from the outer query, so it re-runs per outer row:
```sql
SELECT name, salary FROM employees e
WHERE salary > (
  SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees WHERE department_id = e.department_id
);
```
This finds employees earning above their **own department's** average — a classic interview question.

lessons.title: Set operations
lessons.content:
**UNION** combines results and removes duplicates; **UNION ALL** keeps duplicates (and is faster, since it skips deduplication):
```sql
SELECT name FROM current_employees
UNION
SELECT name FROM alumni;
```

**INTERSECT** returns rows common to both queries; **EXCEPT** (or `MINUS` in Oracle) returns rows in the first query but not the second. Both queries must have the same number of columns with compatible types.

title: CASE Expressions
description: Add conditional logic directly into SQL queries.

lessons.title: CASE WHEN
lessons.content:
`CASE` lets you branch logic within a query, similar to if/else:
```sql
SELECT name, salary,
  CASE
    WHEN salary > 100000 THEN 'High'
    WHEN salary > 60000 THEN 'Medium'
    ELSE 'Entry'
  END AS salary_band
FROM employees;
```
Useful for bucketing data, conditional aggregation, and pivot-style reporting directly in SQL.

title: Views
description: Save reusable queries as virtual tables.

lessons.title: Creating and using views
lessons.content:
A view is a saved query that behaves like a table:
```sql
CREATE VIEW high_earners AS
SELECT name, salary FROM employees WHERE salary > 100000;

SELECT * FROM high_earners WHERE name LIKE 'A%';
```
Views don't store data themselves (with exceptions like materialized views) — they re-run the underlying query each time they're referenced.

title: Indexes — Introduction
description: Understand how indexes speed up query lookups.

lessons.title: Why indexes matter
lessons.content:
Without an index, finding a row requires scanning every row (a **sequential/full table scan**). An index is a separate sorted structure (typically a B-tree) that lets the database jump directly to matching rows.

```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_employees_department ON employees(department);
```

**Trade-off**: indexes speed up reads but slow down writes (every `INSERT`/`UPDATE` must also update the index), and consume additional storage. This trade-off is a common interview discussion point.
